title: How to layer chains without looking messy
excerpt: Length, weight, and finish — the three rules that separate intentional stacking from visual noise.

sections.heading: Start with length separation
sections.body: The number one stacking mistake is choosing chains with identical or near-identical lengths. They cluster, tangle, and read as clutter rather than intentional layering. Separate each chain by at least 2 inches. A classic three-layer stack sits at 16in, 18in, and 20in — each layer gets its own space and its own moment.

sections.heading: Match your finishes
sections.body: Mixed metals can work but require intention. If you're wearing a black PVD chain necklace, your ring and bracelet hardware should match. Silver on silver reads crisp. Gold on gold reads warm and editorial. Mixing randomly reads unfinished. Pick a finish and commit to it across every visible piece.

sections.heading: Vary your chain weight
sections.body: A good stack has contrast in weight, not just length. Pair a flat, light cable chain with a chunkier curb or figaro link. The weight difference creates visual texture and keeps the eye moving. A stack of three identical chains in different lengths is technically correct but visually boring.

sections.heading: Anchoring the look
sections.body: Every great stack has one dominant piece — the anchor. Everything else supports it. If your anchor is a thick curb chain at 18in, keep the secondary and tertiary chains lighter and thinner. The anchor is the focal point; the other pieces are the frame. Never let two heavy chains compete at the same length.

sections.heading: When to stop
sections.body: Two or three chains almost always beats four or five. Restraint is the hallmark of premium styling. If you're asking yourself whether to add another piece, the answer is usually no. Negative space — the skin between layers — is what makes each chain readable and valuable-looking.

cta.heading: Build your stack with Calistique.
cta.body: The Obsidian Chain Necklace and Layered Chain Set are designed to pair cleanly — different weights, same finish.
